stimuli.description: Red stop-signal arrow (left or right) that replaces the white go arrow after the stop-signal delay; participant must withhold the response.
White go arrow (left or right). Left arrow -> ArrowLeft, right arrow -> ArrowRight. Appears on every trial; on stop trials it is later overwritten by the red stop arrow.

phase_detection.feedback: /Average response time|next block starts|Press space to continue|GO TRIALS|STOP-SIGNAL TRIALS/.test(document.body.innerText||"")

citations.authors: Dóra Matzke, Conor V. Dolan, Gordon D. Logan, Scott Brown, Eric‐Jan Wagenmakers
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions.
citations.rationale: Matzke et al. (2012) build their SSRT-estimation method on the explicit assumption that go RTs are ex-Gaussian distributed, validating the choice to model the go-trial RT distribution with an ex-Gaussian mu component.
citations.abstract_snippet: The cognitive concept of response inhibition can be measured with the stop-signal paradigm. In this paradigm, participants perform a 2-choice response time (RT) task where, on some of the trials, the primary task is interrupted by a stop signal that prompts participants to withhold their response. The dependent variable of interest is the latency of the unobservable stop response (stop-signal reaction time, or SSRT). Based on the horse race model (Logan & Cowan, 1984), several methods have been

citations.authors: Manjarrez, Elias, DeLuna-Castruita, Angel, Lizarraga-Cortes, Victoria, Flores, Amira
citations.title: Similarity index of ex-Gaussian reaction time signatures
citations.rationale: Manjarrez et al. (2023) treat the ex-Gaussian triad of mu, sigma, and tau as the standard parameterization of RT variability, supporting use of a mu location parameter for the go distribution.
citations.abstract_snippet:
Abstract
                In psychology and cognitive neuroscience, reaction time (RT) series and their ex-Gaussian distributions are commonly used to explore the time course of cognitive processes. This study investigated the hypothesis that successive triads of ex-Gaussian mu, sigma, and tau parameters of reaction-time variability across time can be used to construct a geometrical object we termed ex-Gaussian RT-signature, which could help characterize interindividual differences between congru

citations.authors: Yang, Xiao, Spangler, Derek P., Jennings, J. Richard, Friedman, Bruce H.
citations.title: Cardiac timing and threatening stimuli influence response inhibition and <scp>ex‐Gaussian</scp> parameters of reaction time in a Go/No‐go task
citations.rationale: Yang et al. (2023) fit ex-Gaussian models to Go-trial RTs and interpret tau as indexing attentional lapses (the slow tail), supporting a tau term in the go distribution.
citations.abstract_snippet: AbstractSensorimotor responses vary as a function of the cardiac cycle phase. These effects, known as cardiac cycle time effects, have been explained by the inhibition of cardiac afferent signals on information processing. However, the validity of cardiac cycle time effects is challenged by mixed findings. Factors such as current information processing and affective context may modulate cardiac cycle time effects and account for inconsistencies in the literature. The current study examines the i

go.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the standard family fit to stop-signal go RTs; parameters yield a ~525 ms mean go RT consistent with published norms for keyboard arrow-direction go responses.

citations.authors: Dóra Matzke, Conor V. Dolan, Gordon D. Logan, Scott Brown, Eric‐Jan Wagenmakers
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions.
citations.rationale: Matzke et al. (2012) model the stop process (SSRT) as ex-Gaussian distributed and develop a method to recover the entire SSRT distribution, supporting an ex-Gaussian mu for the stop/SSRT distribution.
citations.abstract_snippet: The cognitive concept of response inhibition can be measured with the stop-signal paradigm. In this paradigm, participants perform a 2-choice response time (RT) task where, on some of the trials, the primary task is interrupted by a stop signal that prompts participants to withhold their response. The dependent variable of interest is the latency of the unobservable stop response (stop-signal reaction time, or SSRT). Based on the horse race model (Logan & Cowan, 1984), several methods have been

stop.rationale: The stop condition's distribution governs signal-respond (failed-inhibition) RTs, which the race model and empirical data place faster than go RTs; an ex-Gaussian with reduced mu and tau reproduces this ordering.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Two slowing phenomena are documented in stop-signal tasks. (1) Choice errors on go trials produce post-error slowing on the subsequent go trial (~25-60 ms). (2) A stop signal on the prior trial (success or failure) produces a larger transient slowing of the next go RT (post-stop-signal slowing, ~40-90 ms), reflecting proactive caution after an inhibition demand. The interrupt trigger is marked exclusive so a trial following a stop signal is not double-counted as both an error and an interrupt.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Lag-1 pair modulation captures congruency-sequence-type dependencies between a prior and current condition. The stop-signal task has no congruency/conflict dimension (conditions are go vs stop, not congruent vs incongruent), so the literature documents no such pairwise condition-transition modulation; disabled.

citations.authors: D. L. Gilden, Thomas L. Thornton, M. W. Mallon
citations.title: 1/ f Noise in Human Cognition
citations.rationale: Gilden et al. (1995) show that sequences of human estimation errors fluctuate as 1/f noise, evidencing systematic trial-to-trial temporal dependence (autocorrelation) in cognitive performance series — the general phenomenon this phi parameter approximates with a lag-1 AR term. No numeric phi is given in the abstract.
citations.abstract_snippet: When a person attempts to produce from memory a given spatial or temporal interval, there is inevitably some error associated with the estimate. The time course of this error was measured in a series of experiments where subjects repeatedly attempted to replicate given target intervals. Sequences of the errors in both spatial and temporal replications were found to fluctuate as 1/f noises. 1/f noise is encountered in a wide variety of physical systems and is theorized to be a characteristic sign

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT series in speeded keypress tasks, including stop-signal go RTs, show robust short-lag positive autocorrelation (slow-drift / 1-f structure). A modest phi~0.2 reproduces this dependency without overstating it.

fatigue_drift.rationale: The protocol is one practice block plus four short experimental blocks; the literature does not document reliable monotonic within-session RT lengthening (fatigue) for stop-signal tasks of this length. Disabled to avoid an unsupported effect.

condition_repetition.rationale: Direction (left/right) is balanced and not a modeled condition here; go-vs-stop repetition effects beyond the post-stop slowing already captured by post_event_slowing are not separately documented. Disabled to avoid double-counting.

pink_noise.rationale: Low-frequency RT structure is represented via the autocorrelation mechanism; enabling pink_noise as well would double-count the same drift component. Disabled.

practice_effect.rationale: A dedicated practice block precedes the experimental blocks and the staircase adapts SSD; the literature does not specify a stable cross-block RT-decay magnitude for this short design. Disabled rather than assigning an unsupported magnitude.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrement (increasing variability/omissions over time) is documented for long sustained-attention paradigms, not for a four-block stop-signal task. Disabled.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Healthy-adult between-subject variability on stop-signal tasks: mean go RT varies across individuals by ~70 ms SD, with smaller condition-specific spread (~20 ms). sigma/tau are scaled by a 0.8-1.2 multiplier to vary distribution shape across simulated participants. Go-trial accuracy is high and tightly clustered (accuracy_sd 0.02), clipped at 0.85-1.0 since go accuracy below ~85% is atypical for an unambiguous arrow-direction discrimination; omission rate is low (sd 0.01) and clipped at 0.0-0.08.

citations.authors: Eliza Congdon, Jeanette A. Mumford, Jessica R. Cohen, Adriana Galván, Turhan Canli, Russell A. Poldrack
citations.title: Measurement and Reliability of Response Inhibition
citations.rationale: Congdon et al. (2012) establish that response-inhibition RT measures reflect reliable, quantitative interindividual variation in brain function, supporting the existence of between-subject differences in mean RT/latency that this jitter parameter models. The abstract gives no numeric SD in ms.
citations.abstract_snippet: Response inhibition plays a critical role in adaptive functioning and can be assessed with the Stop-signal task, which requires participants to suppress prepotent motor responses. Evidence suggests that this ability to inhibit a prepotent motor response (reflected as Stop-signal reaction time (SSRT)) is a quantitative and heritable measure of interindividual variation in brain function. Although attention has been given to the optimal method of SSRT estimation, and initial evidence exists in sup

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'STOP-IT Stop-Signal Task (jsPsych)' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 2 temporal effects.
